segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a detailed anatomical heart model, illuminated against a dark background, as a male voice (Speaker 1) with an authoritative tone explains that "cardiovascular disease and specifically heart failure is the number one cause of death in men and women across just about every demographic." Subtle, electronic background music begins to play. The visual transitions to a dimly lit, abstract mechanical device with tubes and connectors, while Speaker 1 continues, posing a hypothetical about replacing a bad heart with a machine. Black-and-white archival footage then appears, showing a surgical team in an operating room with a 'OPERATING ROOM 1' sign, performing procedures on a patient and handling a glass medical device. During this, a different male voice (Speaker 2) with a narrative, possibly British-sounding accent, describes how "For the last half century, scientists have been working tirelessly to create a machine that can accurately mimic the function of a beating human heart," envisioning devices to combat the world's number one cause of death. The footage shifts to color, displaying a complex heart-lung machine with rotating parts and tubes, as Speaker 1 returns to discuss the "sigmoid adoption curve" of technology. The scene then moves to a modern laboratory where a man in a white lab coat, with 'European Patent Office' text visible, examines a small mechanical component under a magnifying lamp, as Speaker 1 highlights how recent technological advancements allow for devices that could last a decade or more. The video then shows a man in casual attire holding a small, intricate mechanical heart model, followed by a robotic arm with a similar device attached, while Speaker 1 concludes that a practical, permanent total artificial heart would be one of the biggest accomplishments in modern medicine. The segment ends with a title card displaying 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' and 'Presented By Lilly A MEDICINE COMPANY', surrounded by animated pills, as the electronic background music fades out.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a title card displaying 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' presented by Lilly, surrounded by animated pills and capsules, as gentle, slightly melancholic background music begins. Archival footage then shows a black-and-white surgical team in an operating room, performing a procedure, while a male narrator explains that the only gold standard for heart failure is replacing the failing heart with a healthy donor. The scene transitions to a modern-day shot of a person in scrubs carrying a white 'Playmate Plus' cooler, followed by a black screen with animated graphics illustrating the severe global shortage of heart transplants: 6,000 performed annually versus 1,000,000 requested worldwide and 600,000 in the U.S., as the narrator details these statistics and the background music becomes slightly more tense. William Cohn, MD, a cardiothoracic surgeon at Texas Heart Institute, then appears in a museum-like setting with anatomical models, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, authoritative voice, explaining that the main limitation to transplantation is the availability of donor hearts. As he walks through a hallway, passing a glass display case containing medical artifacts, the narrator introduces him as an innovator in artificial heart technology, accompanied by a subtle mechanical whirring sound. Archival footage reappears, showing a surgeon in a lab coat working on a patient, while Dr. Cohn elaborates on the rarity of finding a suitable donor heart. A close-up of a complex mechanical device with tubes and connectors, likely part of a heart-lung machine, is then shown as Dr. Cohn emphasizes the critical need for alternatives to transplanted hearts. The segment concludes with a vintage animation of a human body with a highlighted circulatory system, transitioning to a full-body silhouette with glowing points, symbolizing the integration of artificial systems, as Dr. Cohn describes the heart as 'just a pump' that supplies nutrition and oxygen before the audio fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a vintage animation of a human body, highlighting the circulatory system with glowing points, transitioning to a detailed anatomical heart model. William Cohn, MD, a cardiothoracic surgeon, is then seen in a modern museum setting, speaking in a clear, informative voice about the heart's function, stating it's 'the hardest to pump that makes the blood move' and 'a very, very simple device by some metrics, but incredibly complex by others.' A subtle, slightly suspenseful electronic background music begins as he gestures toward a display case. The camera focuses on a museum display case showcasing a mechanical heart device with tubes and connectors, labeled 'THORATEC,' alongside informational plaques. Cohn continues, explaining that 'the really first artificial heart was designed by a guy named Domingo Liotta,' a 'brilliant Argentinian scientist' who worked with 'Michael DeBakey, pioneering heart surgeon in Houston.' He details how they 'developed a device that DeBakey had implanted in seven cows,' with the longest lasting 'about 12 hours.' Archival black-and-white footage follows, showing Dr. Denton Cooley, a man in a lab coat, meticulously working on a mechanical heart component under a magnifying lamp. Cohn narrates that while DeBakey was seeking funding, his partner 'Denton Cooley implanted it in a desperately ill human patient, Haskell Karp.' The video then cuts to a vintage color clip of a surgical team in an operating room, performing a procedure on patient Haskell Karp, who is shown lying on the operating table, intubated and monitored, with a mechanical heart device connected to his chest. A heart-lung machine and a graph recording vital signs are visible, and the patient's face shows signs of distress as medical staff monitor the procedure. Cohn concludes, stating this event on 'April 4th, 1969' was 'the world's first clinical use of a total artificial heart.' A different, more formal archival voice then declares, 'The possibility has now been established...', as the background music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with archival footage from April 1969, where a man in a white shirt and tie, identified by an overlaid text as 'Network News Film April 1969', speaks directly to the camera with a formal, slightly dated broadcast voice, reporting on a patient named 'Mr. Karp' whose regained organ function indicates a 'mechanical heart' is a 'feasibility.' The scene then transitions to close-ups of dark rectangular medical components, followed by a surgical view of a heart undergoing an open-heart procedure with tubes and clamps, and a medical monitor displaying a faint signal. Over these visuals, William Cohn, MD, narrates with a clear, calm voice, explaining that the early artificial heart's valves were 'breaking up the blood,' leading to kidney failure and, tragically, the patient's death from infection 36 hours after an emergency transplant, despite 64 hours on the device. Cohn then appears in a modern, museum-like setting, gesturing as he speaks beside a glass display case containing an anatomical heart model, emphasizing that 'it showed us that that type of thing was possible.' A brief, subtle background music plays before the video returns to surgical close-ups of a mechanical valve being inserted into a heart and a detailed view of a white, spherical artificial heart component. Cohn continues his interview, using hand gestures in a well-lit educational space, detailing how the heart had 'bladders that filled and emptied with compressed air that drove the blood forward,' using 'one-way valves' to manage blood flow, concluding that 'that device continued to evolve' based on this technology.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a white mechanical heart device, labeled 'SynCardia', resting on a red surface, as a male voice (likely William Cohn, MD) describes its evolution into the 'world's first approved device,' highlighting its improved membranes, valves, and manufacturing techniques compared to earlier implants. A smiling woman in a red jacket and black pants then walks through a hospital corridor with a backpack and cane, symbolizing independence. This is followed by a detailed anatomical heart model and a mechanical heart device being tested in a lab, with 'European Patent Office' text visible. William Cohn, MD is seen speaking in a museum-like setting as the male voice continues, announcing the CARMAT device's approval for commercial sale in Europe, though emphasizing its 'temporary use.' A hand holds a white, spherical artificial heart component, preceding scenes of an ICU patient with medical staff and a surgical team performing a procedure. A hand connects a small black CARMAT device to a belt, illustrating its portable nature. A different male voice then explains that SynCardia and CARMAT are the only approved artificial hearts, serving as 'bridge to transplant devices' to keep patients alive until a donor heart is available, noting the longest an artificial heart has lasted is '1374 days, or roughly four years,' with 'long-term durability' being the main limitation. William Cohn, MD reappears briefly in the museum setting, and the segment concludes with a man attaching a black portable medical pouch to his belt, connected via a cable, demonstrating a wearable system, while a third male voice explains that a heart beating '80 times a minute' equates to '132,000 times in a day.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with William Cohn, MD, seated in a museum-like environment, speaking thoughtfully about the challenge of artificial hearts wearing out, noting that anything that has to flex "42 million times in a year" and "never fails" is a "tall order." His calm, articulate voice accompanies his gestures. The scene transitions to a black-and-white archival clip showing a dimly lit mechanical apparatus with tubes and connectors, possibly an early medical machine, accompanied by a distinct mechanical whirring and humming sound. The video returns to William Cohn in a close-up, with a colorful anatomical heart model visible in the background, as the mechanical sound fades. A narrator's voice then begins, explaining that "the durability factor has led other groups to rethink their approach." The setting shifts to Brisbane, Australia, where a man walks toward a building labeled 'Pathology Queensland' and 'Clinical Sciences,' carrying a black case, as the narrator introduces "BiVACOR, a startup from Brisbane, Australia, that's headquartered in Houston, Texas." Inside, he enters a modern laboratory where a complex mechanical device, resembling a heart-lung machine with tubes and a rotating component, is being operated. The narrator continues, stating BiVACOR "has come up with a device that hardly resembles a human heart at all" and has raised over $20 million, winning over "industry heavy-headers like Dr. Cohn, who serves as their chief medical officer." William Cohn is then seen walking through the lab, observing the equipment. The scene cuts to Daniel Timms, Chief Executive Officer & Chief Financial Officer of BiVACOR, seated in the lab, who introduces himself as "Daniel Timms," a mechanical engineer with a PhD in biomedical engineering. He states in a clear, informative voice that he "invented the BiVACOR device and have been developing that device ever since." He then holds a small, metallic, spherical artificial heart component, examining it closely. The video concludes with a 3D animation showing the artificial heart component being integrated into a human heart, highlighting its placement and function within the circulatory system.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a detailed 3D animation of the 'Bivacor' mechanical heart device, showcasing its internal components as they disassemble and reassemble in a clean, white environment. The animation highlights the device’s dual-chamber structure with blue and red fluid flows representing blood circulation, and a central rotating impeller mechanism, zooming into copper coils, green insulating layers, and a gold-colored rotor. A male voice, identified as Daniel Timms, explains that "the artificial heart that we're creating is what's called a rotary total artificial heart," featuring a "spinning disk" that is "magnetically levitated" within a cavity to pump blood to both the lungs and the body simultaneously, similar to a fan but pumping blood. He emphasizes this approach is "radically different from utilizing compressing sacks," as the magnetically levitated spinning disk means "there's no touch down of any of those moving parts," allowing it to "effectively last forever." The animation returns, showing a top-down view of the device’s internal flow dynamics with red and blue particles. The video then cuts to real-world footage: a close-up of a white catheter-like tube being manipulated, followed by a gloved hand holding a small, metallic, turbine-like component. An animated diagram illustrates the device’s integration into a human heart. A narrator then states, "Bivacor's artificial heart works off a rapidly spinning pump technology," developed by scientists Rob Jarvik and Rich Wampler in the mid-1980s, whose device used "small turbines sat outside unhealthy hearts." The narrator concludes that "Timms's device builds on that idea by using a spinning disk or impeller," as Daniel Timms is shown holding the actual Bivacor device in one hand and its gold-colored rotor in the other, demonstrating the physical components in a well-lit, professional environment, with the on-screen text 'Bivacor' visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Daniel Timms, a man in a blue shirt, carefully holding and rotating a small, gold-colored, multi-bladed Bivacor Rotor, showcasing its intricate design and concentric grooves. A male narrator explains that this 'impeller' is designed to push and regulate the flow of blood to both ventricles, describing it as a single disk with distinct veins on one side for pumping blood to the body and on the other for the lungs, tailored to different pressure requirements. This setup features transparent vertical tubes, labeled 'Pulmonary Artery' and '283mm Hg', filled with liquid and connected to a network of valves and pumps, simulating a circulatory system. The narrator confirms that 'Bivacor is currently testing its device in this system that circulates liquids to replicate the functions of the human body,' before raising a significant question about future human trials and Bivacor patients.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Daniel Timms in an interview setting, seated on a black leather couch, concluding a thought about getting the device "into those patients that need it." The scene transitions to a close-up of the Bivacor Device (Physical) integrated into the Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus, where a distinct mechanical whirring and pumping sound begins, simulating a circulatory system. Two men, one in a gray sweater and another in a blue shirt, are then shown collaborating at a workstation with dual monitors. A male narrator's voice explains that Bivacor is "currently working with the FDA, undergoing durability and animal studies," and is "poised for their first human implant to take place in 2022." The footage then cuts to a Bloomberg news article highlighting the European approval of the first total artificial heart, featuring a Carmat employee. The narrator continues, stating that "France-based Carmat received approval from the EU at the end of 2020, and has started manufacturing its hearts for commercial sale," with hopes that "Carmat's heart will prove longer lasting than earlier artificial hearts" and become "a permanent replacement, not just a stopgap." This is followed by a dark lab scene labeled 'European Patent Office,' showing a CARMAT Device being tested on a mechanical platform, with a tablet interface displaying real-time data, including French labels. As a subtle, serious background music track begins, the narrator shifts to discussing a "big remaining downside": the "briefcase-sized power packs that have to stay permanently connected to the hearts through the patient's skin." The scene shifts back to the lab with the Bivacor Device, then to a 3D animation of the Bivacor device inside a human torso, followed by a SynCardia device on a red surface, and a man in a blue shirt and boxing gloves training with a punching bag, wearing a portable medical pouch connected to a device. The narrator emphasizes that "advancements like extending battery life and replacing cable drive lines with wireless charging technology would have to be realized before an artificial heart patient's freedom is truly..." as the audio fades. The final shot shows Daniel Timms in a dark blazer, holding the Bivacor Device (Physical) and a red anatomical heart model, demonstrating the device’s connection to the human heart, with the robotic arm and testing apparatus visible in the background.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with Daniel Timms in an interview setting, seated on a black leather couch in a modern indoor space with a glass partition reflecting lab equipment behind him. The scene transitions to a close-up of the Bivacor Device (Physical) integrated into the Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus, with transparent tubes and valves simulating a circulatory system. Two men, one in a gray sweater and another in a blue shirt, are shown collaborating at a workstation with dual monitors displaying complex software interfaces, likely controlling or analyzing the device. The footage then cuts to a Bloomberg news article highlighting the European approval of the first total artificial heart, with a photo of a Carmat employee holding a beige, perforated artificial heart. This is followed by a dark lab scene labeled 'European Patent Office,' showing a CARMAT Device being tested on a mechanical platform with a gold-colored, perforated component. A tablet interface displays real-time data, including pressure waveforms and patient metrics, with French labels such as 'Pression relative ventriculaire gauche (mmHg)'. The scene shifts back to the lab, where the Bivacor Device is shown in full context with the Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus, and a man in a blue shirt is seen working in the background. A 3D animation of the Bivacor device is shown inside a human torso, demonstrating its integration with the circulatory system. The video then features a SynCardia device on a red surface, followed by a man in a blue shirt and boxing gloves training with a punching bag, wearing a portable medical pouch connected to a device. The final shot shows Daniel Timms in a dark blazer, holding the Bivacor Device (Physical) and a red anatomical heart model, demonstrating the device’s connection to the human heart, with the robotic arm and testing apparatus visible in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern laboratory, where a white robotic arm with articulated joints, identified as the Robotic Arm with Mechanical Heart, precisely manipulates a Catheter-Like Tube within a Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus. Simultaneously, Daniel Timms, in a dark blazer, demonstrates the Bivacor Device (Physical) by holding a red anatomical heart model, illustrating its integration with the apparatus via white tubing. Throughout this, a male voice, later identified as William Cohn, MD, begins to speak over a light, ambient, and slightly futuristic background music, discussing the potential for a 'healthy transplant recipient.' The scene then transitions to a close-up of a computer monitor displaying real-time data from the Bivacor device, including graphs for 'Pressure Sensors' and 'Flow Sensors', as a Man in Gray Sweater uses a mouse to control or analyze the data, observed by a Man in Blue Shirt (Workstation). William Cohn's voice continues, noting that 'we're still in this low phase,' with '4 or 5 thousand pumps implanted a year,' contrasting this with '400,000 people dying of heart failure.' The setting shifts to a museum-like environment where William Cohn, MD, now visually present in a blue blazer, speaks animatedly and gestures, with a Museum Display Case containing anatomical models behind him. He elaborates on 'a whole bunch of technical forces that are converging on having an artificial heart that lasts a long time, that doesn't have a wire through the skin,' predicting that 'heart failure won't be the number one cause of death in the world.' The video concludes with end credits on a black background, featuring the 'Lilly' logo, animated pills and capsules, and production roles, followed by a teal 'O' logo, as the background music becomes more prominent before fading out.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment begins in a modern laboratory setting, where a robotic arm with articulated joints (Robotic Arm with Mechanical Heart) is shown interacting with a fluid dynamics testing apparatus, manipulating a catheter-like tube within transparent vertical tubes filled with liquid. A man in a dark blazer (Daniel Timms) holds a red anatomical heart model, demonstrating integration with the Bivacor Device (Physical), which is connected to the apparatus via white tubing. The scene transitions to a close-up of a computer monitor displaying real-time data from the Bivacor device, including graphs for 'Pressure Sensors', 'Flow Sensors', 'Pump Controls & Data', and 'Pressure-Volume Loops', with a man in a gray sweater (Man in Gray Sweater) using a mouse to control or analyze the data alongside a man in a blue shirt (Man in Blue Shirt (Workstation)) observing. The setting then shifts to a museum-like environment, where William Cohn, MD, seated in a chair, speaks animatedly, gesturing with his hands, with a glass display case (Museum Display Case) containing anatomical models behind him. The video concludes with end credits on a black background, featuring the 'Lilly' logo, animated pills and capsules, and text listing production roles, followed by a teal 'O' logo on a dark blue background.
